evidence_text: Augment reports a 65.4% success rate on SWE-bench Verified.
source_excerpt: “Today, we are proud to share that we have achieved a 65.4% success rate on SWE-bench verified.”
note: This is a self-reported benchmark result from the source.
caveat: This is a self-reported benchmark result from the source.

evidence_text: The reported 65.4% SWE-bench Verified submission used Claude Sonnet 3.7 as the core driver and OpenAI o1 as the ensembler.
source_excerpt: “To achieve a 65.4% success rate on our first-ever SWE-bench submission we combined Claude Sonnet 3.7 as our core driver, along with OpenAI’s o1 as our ensembler.”
note: This evidence states the model combination used for the reported submission.
caveat: This evidence states the model combination used for the reported submission.

evidence_text: Augment says its main implementation differences from Anthropic’s published SWE-bench setup were inferring the unpublished planning tool and using OpenAI o1 for ensembling.
source_excerpt: “The main deltas in our implementation include figuring out what their unpublished ‘planning’ tool was and using OpenAI’s o1 model as our ensembler.”
note: This is a direct statement of the source’s claimed implementation deltas.
caveat: This is a direct statement of the source’s claimed implementation deltas.

evidence_text: Augment reports that Claude Sonnet 3.7 thinking mode did not help on SWE-bench.
source_excerpt: “We were surprised to see some techniques that didn’t help such as Sonnet 3.7’s thinking mode...”
note: This is a negative finding about a tested technique and creates tension with any assumption that more explicit thinking automatically improves benchmark performance.
caveat: This is a negative finding about a tested technique and creates tension with any assumption that more explicit thinking automatically improves benchmark performance.

evidence_text: A separate “fix regressions” agent produced no net score improvement because it fixed some regressions but also introduced bugs into otherwise correct candidate solutions.
source_excerpt: “We found this direction of research ultimately wasn’t very fruitful either. While the ‘fix regressions’ agent was able to find and fix some regressions, it also introduced bugs into otherwise correct candidates solutions, resulting in no net improvement to the final score.”
note: This evidence preserves both the partial benefit and the offsetting failure mode.
caveat: This evidence preserves both the partial benefit and the offsetting failure mode.

evidence_text: Augment estimates that ensembling can add 3% to 8% on SWE-bench Verified.
source_excerpt: “In addition to this, there is a gain of 3-8% to be had from ensembling techniques.”
note: This is an internal estimate reported by the source, not a separately documented experiment table.
caveat: This is an internal estimate reported by the source, not a separately documented experiment table.

evidence_text: Augment says agent outcomes were unstable enough that any two rollouts sampled over 50 examples could differ in outcome.
source_excerpt: “We found that when sampling any two of our rollouts over 50 examples would have different outcomes between the two rollouts.”
note: The sentence is grammatically rough in the source, but it clearly asserts rollout instability as the reason ensembling can help.
caveat: The sentence is grammatically rough in the source, but it clearly asserts rollout instability as the reason ensembling can help.

evidence_text: For SWE-bench tasks, Augment found “grep” and “find” sufficient, and embedding-based retrieval was not the bottleneck.
source_excerpt: “We explored adding various embedding-based retrieval tools but found that for SWE-bench tasks this was not the bottleneck – ‘grep’ and ‘find’ were sufficient.”
note: This is scoped to SWE-bench tasks, not all production coding settings.
caveat: This is scoped to SWE-bench tasks, not all production coding settings.

evidence_text: Augment says the same “grep” and “find” navigation approach has limitations in real-world use because of ambiguous user inputs and large codebases.
source_excerpt: “However, while this codebase navigation technique works well for SWE-bench, we found that for real world use-cases, this approach to codebase awareness currently has limitations, due to its ability to handle ambiguous user inputs and large codebases.”
note: This evidence creates a benchmark-to-production tension: the method works on SWE-bench but is reported as limited in broader deployment settings.
caveat: This evidence creates a benchmark-to-production tension: the method works on SWE-bench but is reported as limited in broader deployment settings.

evidence_text: The source cites OpenAI’s finding that only 8.4% of SWE-bench Verified problems take an experienced software engineer more than an hour to solve.
source_excerpt: “Finally, OpenAI found that only 8.4% of problems in SWE-bench Verified take more than an hour to solve by an experienced software engineer.”
note: This is presented as a benchmark limitation and should remain attached to any downstream use of SWE-bench difficulty claims.
caveat: This is presented as a benchmark limitation and should remain attached to any downstream use of SWE-bench difficulty claims.

evidence_text: The source states that SWE-bench only includes Python projects.
source_excerpt: “It also only includes Python projects, missing the diversity of languages in actual development environments.”
note: This is a benchmark-scope caveat that limits cross-language generalization.
caveat: This is a benchmark-scope caveat that limits cross-language generalization.

evidence_text: The source argues that Python is easier for agents than Java or C++ because failed-test error messages tend to be more descriptive.
source_excerpt: “Consider how error messages from failed tests tend to be significantly more descriptive in Python than in languages like Java and C++, which makes Python an easier language for agents to work with.”
note: This is an argued limitation stated in the source, not a quantified cross-language experiment.
caveat: This is an argued limitation stated in the source, not a quantified cross-language experiment.

evidence_text: Augment reports that some changes that improved its production agents in qualitative customer feedback did not improve SWE-bench scores.
source_excerpt: “Today, we are proud to share that we have achieved a 65.4% success rate on SWE-bench verified.”
note: This is a self-reported benchmark result from the source.
caveat: This is a self-reported benchmark result from the source.
